prompts.text: Tell us about the funniest thing your family does at holidays.
Describe your ideal Sunday morning, from waking up to lunch.
Walk us through the worst meal you've ever cooked for yourself.
Tell a friend about the last time you got genuinely lost somewhere.
Describe your favorite childhood hiding spot to someone who's never been there.
Talk us through a memorable first impression that turned out completely wrong.
Tell the story of a small disaster on an otherwise normal day.
Recount a moment you laughed at something you weren't supposed to.
Describe an argument you had where you slowly realized you were wrong.
Tell us about the most awkward gift you've ever received.
Walk through the moment you knew a friendship had changed for good.
Describe the last time a stranger genuinely surprised you.
Tell the story of a time you broke a rule and it turned out fine.
Recount a conversation you wish you could redo.
Describe the strangest dream you can actually still remember.
Tell us about a tradition you've kept that no one else in your life understands.
Narrate a near-miss that could have gone really badly.
Tell the story of a time you had to deliver news no one wanted to hear.
Describe a long journey home — the kind where everything went wrong.
Recount a moment you realized something about yourself you didn't want to know.
Describe waking up before sunrise in a quiet house, then your day building from there.
Tell the story of a small thought that grew into a big decision.
Walk us from the quietest moment of your week to the loudest.
Describe a whisper that turned into a shout in your life.
Talk about the slow build of excitement before a trip you really wanted to take.
Recount falling asleep one night and being startled awake before dawn.
Tell the story of a small disagreement that escalated, then settled.
Describe the experience of standing in an empty stadium and imagining it full.
Walk us through a surprise party from setup to reveal to wind-down.
Tell the story of a quiet idea you had alone that turned into a public moment.
Describe a peaceful walk that ended in unexpected drama.
Narrate the slow arrival, full crash, and aftermath of a thunderstorm.
Tell us about a friendship that began softly, peaked, and then quieted again.
Describe a long road trip that started bored and ended unforgettable.
Walk through the buildup, climax, and quiet end of a memorable celebration.
Tell the story of how you fell in love with something — slowly, then all at once, then steadily.
Describe an argument that started in whispers, peaked in shouts, and ended in stillness.
Narrate a wedding speech from the nervous opening line to the final toast.
Tell the story of grief that arrived quietly, broke open, and softened again.
Describe the rise and fall of a personal obsession over a full year.
Explain to a nervous first-year student why college is worth it.
Tell a younger sibling why they shouldn't be afraid of starting a new school.
Describe your closest friend to someone who's about to meet them.
Welcome a new neighbor and tell them what you love about the area.
Tell us about the person who shaped you most growing up.
Talk to a friend who just bombed a job interview and convince them it's okay.
Describe how you would comfort a child who lost their first pet.
Tell your past self at sixteen what they most need to hear.
Toast a friend on their birthday and tell them why they matter.
Explain to a homesick traveler why their feelings are normal.
Welcome someone to your country and tell them what you hope they notice.
Tell a friend going through a breakup what you wish someone had told you.
Describe a teacher who believed in you when no one else did.
Talk to a parent who feels they failed their kid and convince them otherwise.
Tell a stranger on a long flight about the person you love most.
Welcome a new colleague on their first day and make them feel they belong.
Deliver a eulogy for someone who lived a small, quiet, generous life.
Comfort a friend who just learned they can't have children.
Tell an estranged sibling why you still want them in your life.
Speak to a room of foster parents about the work they're doing.
Make the case for why people should read more fiction.
Argue that everyone should learn to cook at least five real meals.
Explain why long walks are underrated as a daily habit.
State the case for keeping your bedroom phone-free.
Argue that handwritten letters still matter.
Make the case for going to bed earlier than you currently do.
Argue that small talk is a serious social skill, not a waste of time.
Explain why most people quit hobbies too early.
Make the case that boredom is good for you.
Argue that adults should keep playing games on purpose.
State why apologizing well is a leadership skill, not a weakness.
Argue that most decisions in life are reversible — and act like it.
Explain why nostalgia is unreliable as a guide to the past.
Make the case that loyalty is overrated when it costs you your integrity.
Argue that most arguments are about something other than what they appear to be about.
State why being on time is a form of respect, not personality.
Argue that public schools deserve more public defense than they get.
Make the case that ambition without rest is a long-term losing strategy.
Argue that the way a society treats its elderly says more about it than its laws.
Defend the position that some friendships should be allowed to end on purpose.
Tell us five things you believe about how to treat people. End each one clearly.
Give us your three rules for a good morning.
Describe what you value in a friend — make each value land.
List the things you absolutely will not compromise on, and explain why.
Tell us what you've learned about money from your twenties.
State your case for what makes a good neighbor.
Describe the kind of person you're trying to become this year.
Tell us, plainly, what you think a parent owes a child.
Lay out the rules you raise yourself by, now that no one else does.
Describe what a healthy relationship looks like to you, point by point.
State what you've stopped tolerating in your life, and what changed.
Tell us, with conviction, what you owe the friends you love.
Lay out the most important lesson each decade of life has taught you so far.
Describe the qualities you trust in a stranger within five minutes.
Tell us what you wish more people understood about loneliness.
State the case for the kind of community you want to live in.
Tell a younger generation what you owe them and what you don't.
Deliver, in plain statements, what you've decided about love after everything.
Lay out your unshakable beliefs about how to die well, and how to live toward that.
State what you'd want carved on a bench in a park you cared about.
What's a question you've never been able to answer about your own life?
Wonder out loud about why some songs hit differently at different ages.
Think out loud about why you remember the things you remember.
Ask yourself why certain strangers stay in your memory for years.
Wonder why some friendships fade and others survive everything.
Talk through what you still don't understand about your own family.
Think out loud about what you'd ask your grandparents if you could.
Wonder why you make the same mistake again, even when you can see it coming.
Ask, out loud, what your ten-year-old self would be confused about in your life now.
Wonder why certain places feel like home even though you've barely been there.
Think out loud about what part of yourself you've never tested.
Ask why you tell some stories about your life and never tell others.
Wonder why you're still drawn to a thing you say you've moved past.
Think out loud about what you'd do if no one were watching for one full year.
Ask, openly, what kind of person you'd be if you'd taken the other path.
Wonder about the version of you a stranger meets at a party for ten minutes.
Think out loud about what you're still hoping someone will say to you one day.
Wonder what your life would feel like if you didn't remember any of your worst moments.
Ask what you'd want to be true of yourself if you only had a year left.
Wonder, openly, whether the people you love know what you actually think of them.
State what you believe makes someone a good friend, and stand by it.
Tell us, plainly, what you think kids today actually need from adults.
Make the case for forgiving someone — and mean it.
Argue that some things are worth losing sleep over.
State what you think love actually is, separate from how it feels.
Say, with no hedge, what you think makes a life well-lived.
Argue that some opinions deserve to be unpopular and held anyway.
Make the case for telling a friend the truth they don't want to hear.
Stand behind your position on whether it's okay to lie to spare feelings.
Defend the decision to walk away from someone you used to love.
Argue that some traditions are worth keeping even when no one remembers why.
State what you believe parents owe their adult children, and what they don't.
Make the case that there are some things money should never be able to buy.
Argue that being misunderstood is sometimes the price of being honest.
Defend an unpopular opinion you actually hold about how people should treat each other.
Stand behind your view on whether people can really change.
Argue, without softening it, what you believe about the responsibility of being well-off.
Defend a moral position you'd hold even if everyone you loved disagreed.
State what you believe is owed between strangers in a society, and mean every word.
Make the case for a hard decision you've made that you still get questioned on.
Talk about your last vacation, then shift to what you're worried about at home.
Describe your best friend, then describe the friend you've drifted from.
Tell us what you love about your job, then what's wearing you down.
Describe your morning, then your night. Make them feel different.
Talk about something you're proud of, then something you're still working on.
Describe what you wanted at twenty, then what you want now.
Start with a happy memory from childhood, then move to one you'd rather forget.
Describe your favorite place, then a place you can't bring yourself to visit again.
Tell us what you love about a family member, then what you've never told them.
Move from describing a wedding you attended to describing a funeral you attended.
Talk about what excites you about getting older, then what scares you about it.
Describe a friendship in its best month, then in its last month.
Tell us about your dream for next year, then your fear about next year.
Describe the version of you in public, then the version of you when no one's watching.
Move from a story about being a guest to a story about being a host.
Tell us what gives you energy, then what drains it. Make us feel the difference.
Start in the loudest room you've ever been in. Move to the quietest. Move to today.
Move from a story about a beginning to a story about an ending to a story about what came after.
Describe yourself at your best, then at your worst, then at your most honest.
Walk through a story with three distinct emotional acts — and let each one breathe.
Convince a child that bedtime matters. Then convince a parent of the same.
Explain what you do for a living to a five-year-old. Then to a grandparent. Then to a peer.
Describe your weekend to a close friend, then to a manager you barely know.
Tell the story of your first job to a teenager about to start theirs. Then to a retiree.
Pitch your favorite hobby to someone who'd love it. Then to someone who'd hate it.
Welcome a stranger to your hometown. Now do the same for someone who grew up there too.
Apologize to a small child you scared. Now apologize to an adult you offended.
Give a pep talk to a nervous friend. Now give the same talk to a skeptical critic.
Explain why you love a book you love — to a fan of the genre, then to someone who never reads.
Tell a story about a mistake you made — to a trusted friend, then to a job interviewer.
Comfort a friend after a loss. Now comfort a coworker after the same kind of loss.
Defend a habit you have — to your doctor, then to your best friend.
Tell us about your week — to your therapist, then to a group at a dinner party.
Talk about money — to a kid, then to a peer, then to your parents.
Share something you're proud of — to a humble grandparent, then to a peer who's competitive.
Describe a hard year — to someone who lived through it with you, then to someone meeting you today.
Give the same toast at a wedding, a retirement party, and a memorial. Adjust everything but the words.
Tell the story of your biggest failure — to a mentor, then to a mentee, then to a stranger on a plane.
Explain a belief you hold — to someone who agrees, then to someone who deeply doesn't.
Deliver the same news — that you're leaving — to a partner, to a boss, and to a parent.
